data.title: British American Tobacco: Why This 6% Yield Remains My Largest Holding
data.description:
It has been a while since I last covered my largest holding by far, British American Tobacco (BTI), to which I'm going to refer as BAT going forward.

For those who didn't follow my previous coverage, I bought the stock at the height of the tobacco bear market a couple of years ago, when the shares offered close to double-digit dividend yields. With the rising share price and valuation, I have downgraded the rating from "Strong Buy" to "Buy".

Momentum investors might disagree with me here, but in my opinion, that's the right approach, especially when it comes to low-growth businesses like tobacco companies. We have to be bullish when prices are low and dividend yields are high, and we become more cautious once the valuation rises.

Whenever investing in "boring" consumer staples, the dividend is a major factor in total returns over the very long term. Naturally, we want our starting yield to be high and the valuation to be low. That way, we not only profit from juicy dividend yields but also from potential multiple expansion.

And that's exactly what happened with British American Tobacco. Total returns have been staggering from its bear market lows, even outpacing the NASDAQ.

But the past is the past, so where do we stand now?

After the correction over the last few weeks, BAT is once again trading at a 6% dividend yield and a P/E ratio of 11.69. It still offers a margin of safety, even though the shares have risen by ~100% from their bear market lows! That's how undervalued the company really was.

My estimate of BAT's intrinsic value is around 14 to 15 times earnings. At that valuation, the shares would yield around 4.6% to 5%. Add conservative low-to-mid-single-digit growth in EPS and dividends, and we'd still arrive at a return that's in line with the markets over the long term.

But here's an important point that's often forgotten in today's exuberant markets: as investors, we don't want to pay fair value; we need to require a margin of safety.

Right now, shares are trading at a 20% discount to my estimate of intrinsic value. It's a large enough safety buffer to issue a "Buy" rating, especially if we consider the resiliency of the tobacco market.

Tobacco Stocks: The Forgotten Safe Haven

In a world where markets are rising from one all-time high to the next, fewer people want to invest in boring but relatively safe companies growing in the single digits. It's only when greed turns into fear, that dividend stocks with reliable cash flows could become the next highfliers. I believe the world is still cyclical, so it's not a matter of if it will happen, but when.

Tobacco stocks, like BAT, have historically been some of the most reliable dividend stocks one could own, despite falling cigarette volumes over the decades.

The secret lies in the weird economics of the tobacco industry. A 10% price hike does not translate into a 10% price increase at retail because of the high government taxes on cigarettes. I described this phenomenon in previous articles like this one.

Also, companies in the sector have some of the highest gross margins of any consumer product. BAT's gross margin is 83%—well above Coca-Cola's 62% gross margin, which is also best in class.

A high gross margin makes BAT less affected by rising input costs. The company can weather weaker demand or inflationary pressures much better than most companies. And it shows already.

Tobacco Companies vs. Consumer Staple Peers

The last two years have not been a great time for many consumer staple peers. A demand slump and rising costs have weighed on the margins and profitability of food and alcohol companies, but tobacco is bucking the trend.

Not only did the share price rise at a time when many peers were struggling, but the underlying profitability remains intact. One could argue that tobacco companies are still some of the most resilient businesses out there. And not too long ago, one could've bought the whole sector at single-digit P/FCF ratios. If that's not a sign that Mr. Market isn't always right, then I don't know what is.

Next-Generation Products

A couple of weeks ago, I downgraded Imperial Brands from a "Buy" to a "Hold" based on valuation concerns alone. It's trading at similar valuation levels as BAT, so why am I more optimistic about the latter? The answer lies in Next-Generation Products (NGP).

While I do expect the tobacco segment to remain resilient over the next decade, as a long-term investor, I tend to think in decades. NGPs are the future of nicotine consumption, and in order to avoid their "Kodak Moment", tobacco companies have to invest in them.

Given its status as a smaller player in the industry with limited ambition to dominate, I have very little hope for Imperial Brands. BAT's NGP portfolio, on the other hand, looks quite promising. I believe the chances are rising that over the long term, BAT could thrive in a world without cigarettes.

The company is constantly innovating at a faster pace in all categories, and with some patience, I believe it might get there. When I started investing in the company, NGPs were 5.6% of total revenues; five years later, they are 14.13%. And if we add the traditional oral segment, total smokeless revenues are 18.20% of revenues.

NGPs turned profitable two years ago, and since then, profits have been growing at a rapid pace. Let's quickly go through the different categories, starting with the most promising one, modern oral.

Modern Oral

For a long time, modern oral had been a double-edged sword for BAT. It was a rapidly growing category in Europe, but the U.S. had been lagging quite substantially. The reason was that BAT had to offer an inferior product in the U.S. due to regulations. All that changed with the release of Velo Plus in December 2024.

Now, the company has arguably the best product in the U.S. market, and it shows in the numbers. I had been predicting this outperformance long before the results finally proved it.

In 2025, Velo Plus ended the year with a 24% volume share, up from just 6.1% at the beginning of the year. Modern oral sales in the U.S. grew by 297%. In the first half of the year, 1.1 billion pouches were sold, and in the second half, 2.4 billion. Sales more than doubled in just half a year!

Velo Plus is a wet pouch with stable nicotine delivery, and most consumers prefer it to the dry pouches of the market-leading ZYN. BAT is now the global market leader in the fastest-growing and arguably most-promising NGP category.

The company expects revenues to grow from the current £1.2 billion to £4.3 billion in 2030. That'd result in a CAGR of 29.08%. This is an ambitious target, but I believe it can be done as long as the competitive pressures from smaller players don't significantly increase from here.

After all, it's a relatively new category, and the segment comes with high gross margins. It doesn't cost much to produce a can of nicotine pouches, and the barriers to entry are relatively low, but it requires expertise to produce the most satisfying product out there. BAT has a century of experience and studies when it comes to nicotine delivery.

There are plenty of smaller brands, but none of them quite "hit" like Velo, according to my own research. Also, as these categories mature, I expect more regulations around the world, which on average should benefit larger players with expertise like BAT and Philip Morris. After all, these companies have decades of experience in highly regulated markets, and they have the financial resources to handle them.

Heated Tobacco

As the largest holding in my personal portfolio, I wish I could report only good news on BAT, but in order to judge the long-term potential of the company, we need to stay objective.

The new heated tobacco devices, glo Hilo and Hilo Plus, have been introduced to several markets in H2 2025. In a previous article, I was skeptical about the potential of these new devices, even though in many regards they offer better features than the market leader, IQOS. So far, it seems that my fears have materialized.

The devices have been out in large markets like Japan, Italy, and Poland, but there is not much chatter about them on social media. When they are discussed, the opinions are mixed. That's not a good sign. It's safe to say glo Hilo hasn't gone viral yet.

What further makes me even more cautious is that data from online retailers like Amazon shows that glo Hilo doesn't sell well. On Amazon Italy, for example, glo Hilo only sold 50 devices over the last month. Italy is one of the largest heated tobacco markets globally.

Both devices seem to be a great improvement from previous generations, but BAT has raised the price of its sticks to IQOS levels. It seems like most consumers are sticking to the market leader, as a cheaper price was the main attraction for using glo.

At this point, it's safe to say that IQOS has built a moat. And if we think about it, it makes sense. If you're a heated tobacco user, the most important thing is wide availability. You want to buy your sticks at every gas station, every convenience store, and every grocery market. Not only that, you want your favorite flavor to be available.

As a former smoker, I can confirm that the worst thing that could happen to a nicotine user is running out of nicotine. As the market leader with over 80% market share, IQOS is more widely available than glo. It's a moat that's hard to break into, and it requires a massively better product to convince IQOS users to switch to glo.

At the pace BAT is innovating, I believe the company might get there, but patience is required. It could take at least one more generation before we see the heated tobacco segment pick up.

Vapour

While heated tobacco is shielded from competition outside Big Tobacco, vaping is not. Here, the company is not only competing against Philip Morris but, more importantly, against hundreds of Chinese companies, which in many cases sell illicit products.

It's not a level playing field, and as long as the Chinese competition is offering huge vapes in all kinds of flavors at very little cost, I don't see BAT winning in this category. The Chinese competition is not solely a phenomenon in the U.S., but is globally present. The only way for BAT to win in the vapour segment is effective and enforced regulation. Over the very long term, I'm quite confident we're going to see exactly that.

The U.S. is the most important market for vaping, and in H2 2025, we saw the early signs of increased regulation and enforcement. Vuse has returned to growth in the U.S., which is an encouraging early sign.

The main reasons for it are stronger federal and state-level enforcement, but the environment remains volatile.

Outside the U.S., the company launched Vuse Ultra, a smart vaping device with a built-in display and a customizable experience. I believe it's an improvement from the previous generation, but at least in Europe and Canada, the competitive pressures from Chinese companies will likely continue weighing on sales.

Since its launch, there has been very little chatter about the Vuse Ultra online, and the sales on platforms like Amazon don't look promising. We need more regulation and enforcement in order for BAT to thrive in the vaping category globally; innovation is not enough. The U.S. has seen a promising start, but the rest of the world has to catch up.

A Few Last Words on NGPs

It's easy to get lost in all the developments concerning NGPs. It's a fast-changing and volatile environment. The most important thing is to keep winning in the fastest-growing and arguably most profitable (on gross margins) category going forward, and that is modern oral. BAT's modern oral business is firing on all cylinders. Not only in Europe, but more importantly also in the U.S., the largest nicotine market globally.

Another important fact is that almost all profits are still generated by combustibles. It's a category that's shielded from outside competition due to regulations. It's proven to deliver stable cash flows despite a long history of falling volumes.

A few setbacks in heated tobacco and vaping do not threaten the investment thesis. Over the long term, BAT has the potential to do well in all NGP categories, but patience is required. The 6% dividend yield at current levels sweetens the waiting time.

Risk Factors

BAT is operating in an industry with falling volumes; if the decline accelerates from here, it could hurt cash flows. Nicotine is highly regulated around the world, and new regulations could lead to lower profitability.

NGPs are the future of the nicotine industry, and the shift toward smokeless tobacco has opened the door for competition. Modern oral and especially vaping are highly fragmented markets, and success in these new categories over the long term is not certain.

Conclusion

BAT's modern oral business is accelerating, and the tobacco cash cow keeps funding buybacks, dividends, and investments in NGPs. BAT has been very resilient at a time when many other consumer staple peers are struggling with shrinking margins and falling volumes.

I perceive BAT to be a recession-resistant business that's increasingly future-proofing its business model. The path is not without setbacks, but over the long term, success is likely.

At a 11.69x P/E, the company is not as cheap as it once was; therefore, I'm not as bullish as I once was. At a 20% discount to its intrinsic value, the shares remain a "Buy", but the air is getting thinner. In order to achieve great total returns with low-growth companies, the valuation paid is of utmost importance.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Year's Most Positive Signal Rallies Markets, But Iran Risk Favors Select Sectors
data.description:
Introduction

The vertical line on a chart is almost always followed by a vertical line in the other direction. Speed kills in both directions." ~ Paul Tudor Jones

The most dangerous thing is to buy something at the peak of its popularity. At that point, all the favorable facts and optimistic interpretations have been built into the price." ~ Howard Marks

Timing matters, and it matters greatly. I have spent the last 35 years trading, researching, and constructing algorithms to identify and leverage the value across fundamental, technical, and behavioral finance models. Of the ten portfolio models designed for optimal portfolio mixes for members to beat the market at Value & Momentum Breakouts, eight come from enhancing well-tested anomaly research in published financial journals. All of the models continue to outperform the S&P 500 in live forward testing here on Seeking Alpha, and again this year.

Whether you are into dividends, ETFs, long-term value, forensic accounting anomalies, CFO insider trades, or aggressive short-term breakout picks, most of what I do is point investors to opportunities here on Seeking Alpha for large short- and long-term gains with a long track record of success based on your risk preferences.

The Year's Most Positive Signal Rallies Markets, But Iran Risk Favors Select Sectors

Our S&P 500 momentum gauges reflect the largest stocks and fund flows in the market and react to the MDA [multiple-discriminant analysis] cycle conditions of all 500 stocks in the index. This ratio of the most extreme positive (Segment 6) and extreme negative (Segment 2) gives us the momentum signal changes daily, weekly, and monthly. The weekly S&P 500 gauges below show the most extreme positive reversal in the multi-year data set, from near max positive lows on March 30th to current highs of positive momentum back to March 28, 2024.

In my March market article, I discussed the best signs for confirming early market bottoms as we approached peak negative MG values in March. Dip-buyers had been adding through the longest negative signal and 5 consecutive S&P 500 weeks of declines since 2022 until a huge capitulation at the end of March:

  • Dip-Buyers Ride Longest Negative Signal Since 2022 To Next Tactical Bottom

As expected, peak negative momentum reversed from the highest negative levels since April 2025. But, unexpectedly, this reversal has been the fastest frenzied rush to return capital to the market in many years. It appears the primary catalyst has been picking the end of the Iran war, now with an unstable and uncertain ceasefire pause between the US and Iran.

This news-driven market cycle began its incredible rally the hour President Trump remarked publicly that "the Iran war may end within 48 hours." That fueled a +2.91% rally in the S&P 500 on March 31st and the largest 3-week market gain in over a year after suffering five consecutive weeks of declines. A few days later, Iran shot down a US fighter jet and rejected any talks for peace. The market moved ever higher in bullish stair-step patterns, and the current ceasefire pause has sustained the record gains so far into April. What comes next is likely more volatility and risk that favors certain trades and certain sectors.

Ordinarily I talk about how to detect the strongest early market bottom using only Sector and Market gauges, as I shared last time:

1. The S&P 500 and Technology gauges turning positive again.

2. A majority of sector gauges turning positive, including a majority of the 3 mega-cap sectors, Communications, Technology, and Consumer Cyclicals.

3. At least 2 consecutive days of market gains and three consecutive days of rising positive gauge values.

Or you could watch how these different gauges come off near bottoms for a rebound into Q2.

Just a few short weeks ago we were talking in our investment group about max negative momentum conditions with all the gauges negative to an extreme, except Energy and the likelihood of some rebound into Q2:

  • Echoes Of 2022: Bear Bounces As ETF Signal Portfolio Leads S&P 500 By Over 23%

Now I am already discussing the risks of market tops in this incredible rush to benefit from the lows caused by record-high Oil prices and the Iran war. This has easily been the fastest reversal from low to high momentum that I can document in more than 5 years.

What Are the Opportunities?

As I say frequently in prior articles, "We can never have a Major Index recovery without the participation of the semiconductor segment." Our market indices are most heavily weighted on mega-cap stocks. Mega-cap stocks are most numerous in the technology sector, and that is most impacted by the semiconductor segment.

The two biggest movers right now are Technology and Energy with high divergence and potential for more large reversals.

The Direxion Daily Semiconductor Bull 3X ETF (SOXL) chart shows the extreme reaction as April began.

As I have highlighted above, the technical indicators are back in high short-term overbought levels that have always been followed with some level of profit-taking.

What makes the Technology sector signal unique is that we have 99 Semiconductor-related stocks in the sector, while we have 395 Software-related stocks, and their returns this year are extremely divided. 15% of the Semiconductors have over $100 billion market cap, while only 2.7% of the Software sector are $100 billion or larger. This contributes to an ongoing skew where fewer larger cap stocks are pushing the market higher while more smaller technology stocks are heading lower.

Obviously the Market index weightings follow the market sizes, with semiconductor giant NVIDIA (NVDA) growing to over $4.9 trillion in market cap now. This is a larger market cap than all the small-cap Russell 2000 stocks combined and more than 30% larger. NVIDIA has become a "Market" index of its own and remains highly positive into April until the next momentum signal change.

Meanwhile, on the software side of the technology sector, we have many stocks languishing near prior-year lows.

State Street SPDR S&P 500 Software & Services ETF (XSW) is suffering another large correction with strong similarities back to 2022 and 2024. The current software selloff is largely attributed to the AI takeover benefiting the chip manufacturers in the same technology sector.

So now you have some idea of the market skew in the technology sector. A majority of the more numerous and smaller-cap software stocks are below their 200-day moving averages at a 3.4:1 ratio, while quite a large majority 2.5:1 of the Semiconductors larger-cap stocks are well above their 200-day moving average.

So How Should We Trade the Technology Sector?

Longer term, the smaller cap software stocks look to have more growth potential in their recent MDA breakout signals.

For example, SoundHound (SOUN) in the software application segment has triggered a positive MDA buy signal across more than 75 tested variables for the first time in 2026. This selection comes from our actively traded Premium portfolio that is up +16.68% YTD trading in 45 fewer trading days than the S&P 500 (SPX) by following the gauge signals shown above.

Additionally, we hold Micron Technology (MU) among the mega-cap semiconductors, watching for another potentially sharp reversal in the same actively traded portfolio. You can see the enormous difference between the two momentum charts of SoundHound AI and Micron Technology.

What Are the Risks That Are Occurring?

Obviously we have the Iran war risk that is adversely impacting oil prices and creating some remarkable market similarities to 2022. I have written about this previously, when energy was the best and only positive-performing sector for the year. We also had a serious financial meltdown that year among some riskier banks. This post below from last month was right before the S&P 500 tested the low of 6353 at the end of March.

Those risks are definitely getting the most attention. However, we have a looming risk of extreme market skew, somewhat discussed in the Technology divergence between small caps and large caps.

S&P 500 Equal vs. Market-Cap Weighted Record Skew

Throughout 2024 I highlighted this chart of the mega skew with concerns and optimism that we would soon break out to a broad rally condition and away from an extreme concentration in the largest mega-cap stocks.

More than a year later, as we enter the second quarter of 2026, we are seeing the skew accelerate again into many of the very largest S&P 500 stocks at a rate that moves the chart below the 1.20 level and back to risks of more large-cap declines like Q1. We certainly could be on the cusp of a small-cap, financial sector, and broad market recovery longer term that produced rapid unwinding of this skew in 2008 and 2020, but not yet.

This leads me back to one of my 2026 forecast articles on Seeking Alpha about the new Fed Chairman and large potential for out-of-favor value stocks to do extremely well.

  • New Fed Chair, QE & Lower Rates: Where The 2026 Piotroski-Graham Value Stocks Are Hiding

Back then Kevin Warsh only had 35% odds of getting nominated for Fed Chairman, but now it appears nearly a foregone conclusion that he will replace Chairman Powell near the end of May. The projected impact may be the same as I was forecasting, with additional rate cuts ahead of us regardless of market conditions by the May time frame.

So Are the Prior Value Stocks Still Good?

The current January Piotroski-Graham value portfolio is up +13.0% YTD in our tracking from January. Adjusted for large dividends, the Piotroski portfolio is up +14.2%, with the Dividend portfolio up +11.5% YTD

These long-term value stocks are intended for 2-year buy/hold measurements from the scholars who originally published the algorithms in the financial literature. One of the Piotroski value stocks I highlighted in my last article in March was, at the time, the worst-performing value stock from January in this long-term portfolio:

  • CFO Insiders Leading The Markets As Value And Dividends Beat Growth

Since then, Harley-Davidson (HOG) has been on a rapid breakout even as the CFO insider anomaly stocks are accelerating through earnings season. Many of us know well from prior studies that CFOs clearly know when their company stock is a good value and when to load up without creating undue attention.

The Harley-Davidson value selection from the January Piotroski-Graham value portfolio went from the worst-performing stock in the portfolio to now up over +12.8% YTD. The best part is that these value selections from well-documented, peer-reviewed algorithms have consistently delivered long-term gains through very challenging markets, as live-tested here on Seeking Alpha for more than 9 years.

In this long-term value portfolio, we leverage published financial algorithms from scholars like Benjamin Graham and Joseph Piotroski, who have well-documented results in the literature of beating the markets across different decades in their proven research. Without going into all the details and prior computational articles, I have combined their algorithms and enhanced them further to release these Piotroski-Graham value portfolios two times a year, in July and January.

Energy is the Biggest Mover With Iran Uncertainty

The Energy sector gauges, most heavily weighted on oil and natural gas, are finally turning negative after 13 weeks positive from the lows last year. As in prior negative sector signals like January 2025, the decline is unlikely to be straight down to new lows but so far has been the most profitable sector trade in our active ETF portfolios, both in bull/bear funds YTD.

Prior to the signal changes two weeks ago, the Energy sector bull funds were up over 78% in Direxion Daily Energy Bull 2X ETF (ERX), shown in yellow on the chart above, and up +35% in State Street Energy Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLE). Now we are riding the negative signals in Energy bear funds that are likely on a path toward longer-term equilibrium in the $70/bbl range.

FINRA cautions investors that:

Exchange-traded funds (ETFs) that offer leverage or that are designed to perform inversely to the index or benchmark they track, or both, are growing in number and popularity. While such products may be useful in some sophisticated trading strategies, they are highly complex financial instruments that are typically designed to achieve their stated objectives on a daily basis. Due to the effects of compounding, their performance over longer periods of time can differ significantly from their stated daily objective. Therefore, inverse and leveraged ETFs that are reset daily typically are unsuitable for retail investors who plan to hold them for longer than one trading session, particularly in volatile markets.

Conclusion

What I offer readers is intended to share different financial models for your future success. We all know that timing matters greatly. Scholars have documented for years that "harvesting different risk baskets, each with a premium that has low-to-negative correlations to the others," is an excellent way to produce strong risk-adjusted returns. These ongoing sector rotations and signal changes continue to give us many new opportunities for profit in the years ahead as we build our optimal portfolio mixes each year.

I always encourage considering three steps in stock selection, whether for long-term value or short-term gains:

  1. Check the direction of the Market Gauges for the largest positive flows.
  2. Identify the Sector Gauges that are most positive and least extended to further enhance probabilities of gains.
  3. Lastly, confirm the positive MDA breakout signals on individual stocks in the best sector and best market conditions to maximize returns.

I wish you well in all your trading decisions,

JD Henning, PhD

data.title: PNC Financial: Not A Great Buy Right Now
data.description:
The PNC Financial Services Group (PNC) exceeded EPS analyst estimates for the first fiscal quarter last week, but missed slightly on revenue expectations. The regional bank completed its transaction with FirstBank in the first quarter, which implies that PNC Financial Services will now be able to expand its balance sheet and grow its deposits and loans, especially in growth areas like Colorado and Arizona.

Additionally, PNC Financial Services benefited from higher net interest income in Q1 '26 -- driven by favorable mix shifts and favorable asset repricing. While inflation recently soared to 3.3% in March, there are indications that the war between the U.S. and Iran may be winding down, which could create an incentive for the Federal Reserve to resume its rate-cutting policy. From a valuation perspective, I believe that PNC Financial Services is about fully valued, and I would recommend investors wait for a drop before establishing a new position in the regional bank.

Previous rating

In my last review on the regional banking platform, I advised investors to hold shares of PNC specifically because of PNC Financial Services' proposed acquisition of FirstBank. The acquisition target at the time was an opportunity for PNC Financial Services to expand its business in the important Denver area, but the risk of potential interest rate cuts counterbalanced my view on the bank: Limited Upside In 2026.

While the Federal Reserve did not lower federal fund rates in March, recent developments in the Middle East suggest that we could see rate cuts in 2026. Despite a healthy pace of loan expansion and higher NII, I see shares of PNC, both historically, as well as, in comparison to other regional banks, as fairly valued.

FirstBank Acquisition makes an impact on PNC Financial in Q1 '26

PNC Financial Services closed the acquisition of FirstBank on January 5, 2026, which ended up adding approximately $15.5B in loans and $22.0B in deposits to the regional bank's balance sheet. FirstBank is a leader in middle-market commercial lending and high-growth retail banking in the Southeast, especially in Colorado and Arizona, and the integration of the bank's retail operations was the primary driver of PNC Financial Services' 7% Q/Q loan growth in Q1 '26.

Overall, PNC Financial Services, despite missing top line estimates, reported impressive results for Q1 '26: the bank's total earnings increased 18% year-over-year to $1.8B, with net interest income (a function of the bank's larger loan operations post-acquisition) being the single most significant driver of earnings growth.

PNC Financial Services' net interest income surged 14% year-over-year to $4.0B, mainly because of higher loan balances in the bank's portfolio. This growth was driven, as mentioned above, primarily by an expansion of the loan portfolio (+11% year-over-year), which is the result of the FirstBank acquisition as well as strong organic loan growth in the commercial category.

Given the bank's current guidance for FY 2026, management sees ~14.5% growth in NII (the bank removed its expectation of rate cuts in 2026 from its projection), so PNC Financial Services clearly expects to benefit from both the current higher-for-longer rate environment as well as new loans being added to the portfolio following the FirstBank consolidation.

Other income sources for PNC Financial Services, especially its fee income derived from its asset management and capital markets advisory business, also increased in the first quarter: here, the regional bank published $2.1B in fee income, showing 13% year-over-year growth. This growth was primarily catalyzed by higher client activity in corporate banking and the structural addition of FirstBank's fee-generating operations.

PNC Financial's valuation

PNC Financial Services went through a lot of turmoil in the last three years, in part because of the regional banking sector meltdown of 2023 -- that was initiated by the collapse of SVB in California at the time -- and because of war fears related to the U.S.'s attack on Iran at the end of February. However, shares have rebounded nicely throughout March and especially April, and they are now at about the same level they traded at before the war broke out.

PNC Financial Services' 1.42X book value multiplier compares to a 3-year average P/B ratio of 1.40X, which means, in a historical context, the regional bank is about fairly valued right now. Further, the industry group average P/B ratio -- which includes the valuation ratios of KeyCorp (KEY) and U.S. Bancorp (USB) -- sits at 1.42X as well, so shares are about fairly valued in a peer-comparison context as well. In other words, investors that are looking for a real bargain in the regional banking market right now will be hard-pressed to find one here with PNC Financial Services.

A 1.42X P/B ratio, applied to PNC's Q1 '26 book value of $143.65 per share, implies a fair value of $204, so PNC Financial Services is trading slightly above my stock price target (Friday's closing price was $224.81). However, the bank just completed its acquisition of FirstBank, which has already been a growth catalyst for the lender as far as its loans and net interest income are concerned. Also, with some new uncertainty about the short-term interest rate path, given the reacceleration of inflation in March, the bank does have upside in its net interest income should the Federal Reserve delay federal funds rate cuts in 2026. My personal opinion here is that the current valuation levels do not indicate either massive undervaluation or overvaluation, so I would settle here for a neutral hold rating.

Risks with PNC Financial Services

The biggest risk that I see for PNC Financial Services relates to the trajectory of its net interest income, which is out of the control of the bank and subject to the interest rate policy of the Federal Reserve. The Fed did not lower federal fund rates last March, mainly due to concerns about energy-driven price inflation, which is good for the bank and its earnings potential in the short term.

Now that Iran has agreed to open up the Strait of Hormuz, however, and no longer blockades the important shipping route -- through which 20% of global oil supplies flow on a daily basis -- energy prices have started to correct sharply to the downside. Lower energy prices could change interest rate expectations and force the Federal Reserve to lower federal fund rates in the medium term, which would negatively impact PNC Financial Services' net interest income outlook.

Final thoughts

PNC Financial Services is a leading regional banking franchise that made significant inroads in new markets in the first quarter due to the acquisition of FirstBank's operations in Colorado and Arizona. While I like the acquisition-driven growth for PNC Financial Services, in terms of both net interest income and fee income, shares of the regional banking platform seem to be about fairly valued.

Since we could be standing on the precipice of peace again in the Middle East, after Iran opened up the Strait of Hormuz last week, I believe that the Federal Reserve will have an incentive to lower federal fund rates in the second half of the year, assuming that inflation cools and approaches the long-term Fed target of 2%. With shares of PNC Financial Services also trading again at their longer-term P/B average, I don't see a reason for investors to buy into the lender at this point.

data.title: Why Onto Innovation Is A Still A Buy After More Than Doubling In 4 Years
data.description:
In 2022, Onto Innovation (ONTO) offered investors an entry point after the stock dipped. In that time, the company rewarded shareholders with an over 220% return. This beat the S&P 500 (IVV), which gained 59.45%.

On April 17, Stifel Financial upgraded ONTO stock to a buy. It set a $350 price target. Will Onto meet Stifel’s price target this year? Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating also raised its rating to a ‘Strong Buy’ on April 17, 2026. Instead of relying on one analyst’s opinion that sent the stock up by 8.8% on the day, investors should assess Onto’s recent fundamental changes.

Mixed Fiscal Fourth Quarter Results

In the fourth quarter, Onto posted a non-GAAP earnings per share of $1.26, meeting expectations. Revenue grew by 1.1% Y/Y to $267 million, barely missing expectations. EPS GAAP actual also missed the analyst forecast. ONTO stock rose from the $200 trading range at the time to close at $290.76 on April 17.

Onto posted record revenue in Q4 and the full year. It acquired several product lines from Semilab International. It shipped several next-generation Dragonfly systems for the high-resolution, 2D applications market. The product achieved a successful qualification on April 16. Onto expects initial shipments in June.

The qualification process completes the process for the Dragonfly G5 system. Features include new proprietary optics, illumination, and algorithms that enhance visibility and throughput. This appeals to customers, since it lowers the overall cost of ownership. Onto cited industry analyst viewpoints on the demand for AI devices. Enabling them would advance packaging, a market that will grow by 30% yearly in the next two years.

Markets rewarded investors who invested in process control. Aehr Systems (AEHR), which provides test and burn-in solutions for the silicon carbide (“SiC”) market, rose eightfold in the last year.

Onto said in its Q4 and full-year press release that “With global AI investment fueling a robust upcycle in semiconductor capital equipment spending, our deep and broad portfolio across advanced packaging, advanced nodes, and specialty devices positions us to capitalize on the high-growth segments of the market.”

Onto is a downstream supplier to the growing AI market. After markets sent NVIDIA (NVDA) to a $4.9 trillion market capitalization, firms “downstream” to AI are rising. I provided coverage for firms benefiting from investments from NVIDIA. They included Nokia (NOK), Ciena (CIEN), Coherent (COHR), and Lumentum (LITE). More recently covered, Ericsson (ERIC), which is not a beneficiary of NVIDIA’s investment, cited its low-cost 5G infrastructure as appealing for AI markets.

Minimal Revenue Growth

Onto citing AI as a growth catalyst is at odds with its 1.1% Y/Y revenue increase. Gross margin also fell to 46.4%, compared to 50.2% in Q4/2024. Non-GAAP gross margin was 54.6%, compared to 54.5% the year before.

Advanced packaging and specialty devices accounted for more than half of Onto’s revenue in the last quarter. Revenue from advanced nodes more than doubled in the full year, lifted by growth in both DRAM and logic (75% of total). Cash generation of $95 million was a record quarter for Onto. The firm applied the One Big Beautiful Bill Tax Act to accelerate the expensing of certain research and development costs.

Stock markets are a forward-pricing machine, so it is assigning a valuation based on Onto’s packaging growth of 30%. Onto CEO Michael Plisinski said that Wafer Fabrication Equipment (“WFE”) sales are hard to track. It recently added advanced packaging orders. Additionally, it needed to include construction costs. Large device manufacturers and outsourcing semiconductor assembly and test firms, along with smaller players in the market, will consider providing alternative solutions. Despite the uncertainties, investors are pricing in an optimistic outlook.

Onto applied its historical capacity to prepare for a $2 billion run rate. By adding extended factories in the next few quarters, its capacity will increase.

Onto Stock Grades

Onto’s quant history rose from a sell/strong sell up until November 2025. Afterward, its rating cycled between a hold and buy/strong buy.

As shown above, Onto stock has held a Buy rating since last month.

In 2026, Onto is the second-best-performing stock. Teradyne (TER) outperformed it, while Lam Research (LRCX), KLA (KLAC), Nova (NVMI), and Applied Materials (AMAT) trailed its performance:

It ranks 15th in the sector and third place in the industry rank. It scores an A on the stock momentum and EPS revisions grades, as do its peers. However, its valuation of “D” is relatively the best.

Investors get a company with strong growth and profitability (graded B and B+, respectively).

Your Takeaway

Markets rewarded momentum traders who bought shares of Onto Innovation. Investors who are seeking value might consider this stock, too. It offers relative value compared to its peers. Just as the breakout in Aehr Test Systems and Lumentum showed no signs of reversing, the same appears true for Onto. Until that uptrend breaks down, Onto earns a reiterated buy rating four years after I initiated coverage on the company.

data.title: Week Ahead: Dollar And Stocks Due For Corrections?
data.description:
Progress in US-Iran negotiations and a ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon fed investor optimism. The US S&P 500 (SP500) and Nasdaq reached new record highs, with multi-week rallies in tow. June WTI tumbled 7.6% last week, after the 8.6% drop the previous week. It briefly traded below $79 a barrel, down from the peak on March 9 (~$104.35). Interest rate expectations also shifted. In Europe, the two-year yields fell by 20-25 bp last week. The US two-year yield fell by a more modest 7-8 bp. Although Japan's two-year yield slipped almost three basis points, there was a more dramatic move in the swaps market, which dramatically adjusted the odds of a rate hike by the Bank of Japan later this month to less than 20% from around 55% a week ago.

Developments in the Middle East may be more important than the high-frequency economic data. The risk is that the market is getting ahead of itself and the situation is likely to be clarified over the weekend. US earnings season features tech and airlines next week. The dollar fell against all the G10 currencies last week but recovered from its worst levels in the waning hours of last week's activity. After rallying last month, the dollar has unwound a good part of those gains. Like the 13-day rally in the Nasdaq is an extreme, the Dollar Index (DXY) has fallen for nine of the past 10 sessions. The rolling 30-day inverse correlation of changes in the Dollar Index and NASDAQ is a little more than -0.60, which is the most since the end of 2023.

US

Drivers: The prospect that the Middle East war can end boosted risk appetites and weighed on the US dollar. The Dollar Index fell for the third consecutive week, matching its longest losing streak in a year. The Fed funds futures market is discounting an almost 65% chance of a cut before the end of the year, the highest probability in a month.

Data: Stronger auto sales and a dramatic rise in gasoline prices likely lifted March retail sales. Excluding autos and gasoline, retail sales may have edged up by 0.1-0.2%. Outside of retail sales, the other US high-frequency data are surveys. April Fed surveys (Philadelphia non-manufacturing survey and KC Fed's manufacturing and non-manufacturing) and the preliminary April PMI will likely pick up some of the disruption from the Middle East war. Q4 25 GDP was reported initially at 1.4% (annualized). It was halved in the first revision and to 0.5% in the second revision. The Atlanta Fed nowcast is for Q1 26 at 1.3%. It will be updated after the retail sales and business inventories on Tuesday. Also, Kevin Warsh's confirmation hearing is scheduled for next week but could be postponed. As long as the investigations into the Fed continue, and the president indicated they would, there appears to be a blocking majority on the Senate Banking Committee. Our understanding is that if a successor has not been confirmed by the end of Powell's term as chair (May 15), he would likely be picked by the board to continue in that post. His term as governor does not end until 2028, and we suspect the odds favor him continuing to serve to protect what appears to be a strong conviction of the importance of the Federal Reserve's independence.

Prices: The Dollar Index fell for eight consecutive sessions through April 15. That matched its longest losing streak in 15 years. It fell through 97.65, its lowest level since the war began, and met the (50%) retracement of the rally from the year's low recorded in late January around 95.55. The Dollar Index recovered through most of the North American session before the weekend and reached almost 98.25. The momentum indicators are overextended but do not prevent additional near-term losses. Still, a move above 98.40-50 would suggest a near-term bottom is in place.

EMU

Drivers: The euro benefitted from the market's stronger appetite for risk and the broad dollar pullback more than a particular European development. At its peak on March 24, the swaps market discounted three rate hikes fully by the ECB this year and about a 25% chance of a fourth. It now has two hikes discounted and less than a 10% chance of a third. This still seems excessive. At its most extreme last month, the swaps market had an 85% chance of an April hike. It has been scaled back to about 12%.

Data: The preliminary April PMI is the highlight. Recall that the composite (output) had begun faltering. It rose from last June through November. It has now slowed three of the four months through March. The general pattern holds for both services and manufacturing. The Middle East war is disruptive on many channels, and it would be surprising if the March data fully reflected the impact. The same is true of Germany's ZEW and IFO April surveys, also due.

Prices: The euro has risen in nine of the past 10 sessions. Ahead of the weekend, it took out the high set before the war began (slightly below $1.1830) and proceeded to sell off around $1.1760. The potential key reversal warns that despite the momentum indicators still rising, the euro's four-cent rally over the past month may be over. If so, the first corrective target may be in the $1.1675-$1.1700 area.

PRC

Drivers: The PBOC has continued its campaign that began last May or June to appreciate the yuan. It has consistently been lowering the dollar's reference rate and now is at a three-year low. The campaign does not appear over even though the end point is not clear. Judging by several bank recommendations, portfolio flows appear supportive.

Data: Chinese banks will set their loan prime rates on April 20. The one-year rate is at 3.0% and the five-year rate is at 3.50%. Before the Middle East war, expecting more monetary easing seemed reasonable, but now it looks less certain. In any event, a clearer signal from the PBOC may be necessary for the loan prime rates to be changed. The increased use of China's International Payments System (CIPS) to settle and clear international transactions would seem to make less relevant, when taken in isolation, the use of the yuan on the SWIFT global payment system. On SWIFT, the yuan's share of payments peaked in July 2024 near 4.75%. In February, its share was about 2.75%.

Prices: The PBOC has continued to allow gradual appreciation of the yuan. In the offshore market, it rose to its best level since February 2023. The market has been encouraged by the central bank's guidance through the setting of the daily reference rate. It was set at its highest level against the dollar since April 2023 last week. The greenback found support near CNH6.80. The CNH6.85-CNH6.86 area may offer a nearby cap.

Japan

Drivers: There are three significant influences on the dollar-yen. The first is the general direction of the dollar. The rolling 30-day correlation between changes in the dollar-yen exchange rate and the Dollar Index is at 0.75. The second is the change in the 10-year US yield. The correlation with the exchange rate is near 0.65, the highest since last October. Third, there is a risk element to the yen as well. The rolling 30-day correlation of change in the exchange rate and the S&P 500 is around -0.55, the most extreme since late 2022.

Data: While Japan has a busy calendar of high-frequency reports, they will likely have minimal impact on expectations for the BOJ meeting. The national March CPI, for example, was anticipated by the Tokyo figures a few weeks ago. Tokyo's year-over-year headline and core CPI measures edged 0.1% lower. The BOJ targets the core CPI rate, and in February, it stood at 1.6%. It was the first time since March 2022 that it was below the 2% target. One of the few things that most international economists might be able to agree on is that the Japanese yen is undervalued. And yet, it has not recorded an annual trade surplus since 2020. The deficit is shrinking. In the first two months of the year, Japan's trade deficit was around half the size of the shortfall in Jan-Feb 2025. Yet it seems like the terms of trade (export/import price indices) are going against it. The preliminary March PMI is due, but the market does not typically respond to it. The February tertiary industry index will help economists forecast GDP, but growth this quarter seems on par with Q4 25's 1.3% annualized. Meanwhile, BOJ Governor Ueda's comments were not strong enough to arrest the decline in expectation of a rate cut at the end of the month. In the past two weeks, the odds of a hike in the swaps market went from around 67% to less than 17%. We had thought it was more likely but recognize that the Bank of Japan will most likely not surprise the market.

Prices: After the dollar was turned back from its approach of JPY160 at the start of last week, it reached JPY157.60 at the end of the week. Ahead of the weekend, the greenback posted an outside down day by trading on both sides of the previous day's range, but it did not settle below its low (~JPY158.25), neutralizing the technical signal. The momentum indicators are falling, and the dollar looks soft. The fact that the dollar fell for the third consecutive week against the yen underscores our argument that despite the finance minister’s comments about being willing to take "bold action," the conditions for material intervention are not present.

UK

Drivers: Sterling benefits from a weaker dollar. The rolling 30-day inverse correlation between changes in sterling and the Dollar Index is almost -0.90, the most in six months. Sterling's correlation with changes in the US two-year yield is -0.33. The inversion is intuitively understandable, higher US rates, weaker sterling. What may be surprising is that the correlation between sterling and changes in the two-year UK yield is also inverse, though slightly (-0.12). Over the last two years, the rolling 30-day correlation has spent more time inverted than positive.

Data: It is an important week for UK data. While data feeds into the central bank's assessment, the bar for a change in policy next week seems high. Updates on the labor market, retail sales, and inflation are due. The UK is experiencing one of the strongest CPI increases and is one of the weakest major economies. The preliminary April PMI is due as well. In March, the composite dropped to its lowest level, 50.3, since last April, when it dipped below the 50 boom/bust level in a bit of a statistical quirk. It jumped back the following month. The data are unlikely to change anyone's mind that the BOE will stand pat even though there may be a dissent in favor of a hike. Recall that the March decision to leave policy unchanged was the first unanimous decision in 4.5 years.

Prices: Sterling stalled around $1.36 last week. Some frustrated late longs look to have bailed and sent sterling back to slightly below $1.3515 in late dealings before the weekend. By settling 1/100 of a cent below the previous day's range, sterling posted a potentially key reversal. It recorded a four-month low at the end of March near $1.3160, and with last week's advance, it met the (61.8%) retracement of the losses from nearly $1.3670, the high since September 2021 in late January. The momentum indicators are overextended, and although they have not turned lower, the month's advance is long in the tooth. A break of $1.3500 could signal scope for another cent decline.

Canada

Drivers: The rolling 30-day correlation between the changes of the US dollar-Canadian dollar exchange rate and the Dollar Index is more than twice as high as the correlation between the exchange rate and oil prices. The correlation with the Dollar Index is around 0.65, gradually rising from the late March low near 0.56. In Q1, the correlation reached nearly 0.85, the highest since May 2024. As counterintuitive as it may seem, since around the middle of last month, the US dollar has tended to appreciate against the Canadian dollar when oil prices are rising. The 30-day rolling correlation jumped to 0.47 last week, the highest since last August. There is also an element of risk. The rolling 30-day correlation is inverse with changes in the S&P 500. Around -0.36, it is the most in four months.

Data: Canada reports March CPI and February retail sales. Canada's headline CPI before the war was 1.8%, and the core stood at 2.0%. Prices are expected to have risen in March. The median forecast in Bloomberg's survey is for the headline rate to jump to 2.6% and for the core rates to edge up. The March jobs report was uninspiring. The market recognizes that the central bank may sit on the sidelines for the next few months. Before the war began, the swaps market was discounting about a 40% chance of another cut (after cutting the target rate by 275 bp over the past two years). At the peak on March 20, the pricing in the swaps market was consistent with a little more than three hikes. Now it discounts one hike fully and almost 10% chance of a second.

Prices: The Canadian dollar has been on a run. It has risen in nine of the past ten sessions for almost a 2% gain. One way to appreciate the magnitude of the move, is to consider that the one-month implied volatility is about 4.5%, annualized. The US dollar reached CAD1.3650 before the weekend, a one-month low. It stalled and recovered to almost CAD1.3690. The greenback is coming down from around CAD1.3965 at the end of March, and the move seems advanced. The risk-reward may be shifting against chasing the Canadian dollar higher. The risks of a greenback bounce appear to be increasing.

Australia

Drivers: The broad movement in the US dollar seems to be the more important driver of the Australian dollar's exchange rate. The rolling 30-day inverse correlation of changes in the Aussie and the Dollar Index is around -0.75, near the most extreme since last September. It is positively correlated with the S&P 500, about 0.65, for the past 30 sessions. It has spent little time above 0.70 since last November. Changes in the Aussie and oil are around -0.40, the most since last September. Counterintuitively, the Aussie is inversely correlated with changes in Australia's two-year yield. It is near this year's low, near -0.22.

Data: The only data of note in the coming days is the preliminary PMI. Recall that in March, the manufacturing, services, and composite readings were all below the 50 boom/bust period. The disruption of the Middle East war likely had more impact than the two rate hikes, with the second taking place after the war began. The composite PMI plummeted to 46.6, the lowest since late 2023, from 52.5 in February. It is the first sub-50 reading since September 2024. Ironically, the manufacturing sector held up better than services. The manufacturing PMI fell for the second consecutive month, and March's 49.8 was the weakest since last October. The services PMI peaked in January at 56.3. It fell sharply in February to 52.8 and then fell nearly twice as much in March to 46.3, signaling the deepest contraction since November 2023. The central bank meets on May 5. The futures market has discounted almost 80% of another hike. We had leaned against the third consecutive rate hike, but the prospects of an end to the war in the Middle East and hawkish official comments forced a re-evaluation.

Prices: The Australian dollar reached its best level since mid-2022 at the end of last week. It poked above $0.7220 to culminate the three-week rally of a little more than 4.5%. It frayed the upper Bollinger Band (~$0.7215) but pulled back to settle around $0.7175. Initial support may be in the $0.7130 area, and a break could signal a corrective phase that could extend toward $0.7000.

Mexico

Drivers: The Mexican peso appears most sensitive to the risk environment. Changes in the dollar-peso exchange rate and the S&P 500 are around -0.80. This is the most extreme since 2020. Ironically, the changes in the USD against the peso and changes in the Australian dollar are almost -0.80. The correlation of the exchange rate and the Dollar Index is about 0.75, the most since last September. Rising oil prices are not helpful for the peso. The rolling 30-day correlation of changes in WTI and the dollar-peso exchange rate is a little below 0.50, a four-year high.

Data: Mexico's February retail sales are too dated to have much impact on the central bank's decision early next month. It is difficult to see it sustaining momentum after the 1% surge in January. The February IGAE economic activity report may not be particularly helpful either, given the disruption from the war. Mexico will also report March unemployment, which stood at 2.59% in February. It recorded last year's low at 2.22% in March, a record under the current time series that began in 1994. Remember, though, that over half of Mexican workers are in the informal economy.

Prices: The dollar initially was sold to a new low since the Middle East war began ahead of the weekend, reaching almost MXN17.1275. However, it reversed higher and closed above Thursday's high (~MXN17.2960). This is the kind of price action one looks for at the end of a sustained move. The MXN17.45-50 area may offer initial resistance.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Buy 5 Ideal 'Safer' Dividend Dogs Of 40 March ReFa/Ro
data.description:
Reader Selections

Since May, 2017, any dividend-paying stock mentioned in a message, e-mail or comment to the author has been fair game for a REader FAvorite listing in this series of articles. Thus, It is possible that only ROgues and discontinued, or dreadful, doubtful, dividend issues may appear.

Reader comments that catch errors in calculations or changes in direction, and figures to communicate are most valuable.

However the main subject of these articles are your stock ticker comments whether they be about favorites or rogues. Be sure to mention a ticker when you comment.

The more tickers mentioned, the better these articles become.

The “safer” lists are now available on the regularly published side of Seeking Alpha but that has added to confusion, since my IDEAL stocks come in two flavors: (1) not so safe and (2) safe, which is preferable since the dividends are paid from cash flow, not borrowed money.

In June a reader asked if Qualified dividend stocks could be flagged. YCharts is taking that request up with their product development team. YCharts, says: “At this time we do not have a filter or screener criterion to identify stocks that explicitly pay "qualified dividends" per IRS standards. However, I am happy to pass along your feedback to our team if this is something that can be added on. I can't guarantee any changes, but they use feedback like that to help determine what enhancements should be added in future updates.”

My ten (going on eleven) year collection of Dogs of The Week portfolios are reserved for members of my Seeking Alpha Dividend Dogcatcher investing group subscribers and a celebratory anniversary book is planned.

Still, readers complain they can’t find my eleven Dog of the week portfolios in my Dividend Dogcatcher service on the SA Marketplace (now called Investing Groups) site.

Summary and reference guides to 11 portfolios:

The Elephant Folio (DOTW XI), initiated October 10, 2025 will be filled with 52 member stocks in October 9, 2026, and be fully funded by October 8, 2027.

Stay tuned for the first newly formatted Toy Dog Portfolio of tiny but mighty low priced dividend dogs.

Last winter a reader asked what the XN rating in the Market Cap column of my Yield and Target Price charts means. Here is my summary of those codes:

Dogcatcher Market Cap Codes

L= $10B+

M= $2B-$10B

S= $200M-$2B

XN=<$200M

Your comments count and encourage better writing by the Dogcatcher

In May. 2025 a reader, named Move on, commented on my Bloomberg Focus List article: “Death by data…just get to the point already…

My reply: @Move on Good point! Here's a hint: Forget the text. Read the charts and graphs. Those are the dots to connect.

No doubt, less is more! I’ll be alert for methods to make my Dividend Kings, Dow Dogs, Aristocrats, Fortune 500 Industry Leaders, MoPays, and all my other articles, less opaque.

In February, 2022 one reader suggested an option strategy for monthly-paying dividend stocks:

"You should identify where options are available on the Monthly dividend stocks. What I do is find mo-pay stocks with options, I buy and write covered calls about 6 - 12 months out. I look for a scenario where I collect the dividend and get my stock bought back at a much lower price than I pay, but pocket a premium that makes up the loss. This gives me a dividend boost, since my cost is lower. It's like a guaranteed CD with little risk."

More than one writer has decried my favoritism for low-priced stocks. They especially dislike my "ideal" stocks whose dividend returns from a $1k investment equal or exceed share price.

A prime example is Sirius XM Holdings Inc (SIRI); the satellite radio and pandora music catalog owner, spun off from Liberty media (and now listed as one of the S&P500).

A reverse-split re-priced SIRI in October 2024. Recently (4/17/26) priced at $25.50, SIRI still easily passes my test (of dividends from $1k invested exceeding share price) with a forward dividend yield of 4.37%! A little over $1k investment now buys 39.22 shares. and they'll throw the owner a return (from that $1k invested) over 1.66 times the share price. And more good news, SIRI is out the doghouse this month because of positive returns for the year to date of 25.21%.

Assuming all things remain equal, SIRI dividends alone will pay back their purchase price in about 23.6 years (and that assumes the satellite radio and subscription music services can survive that long)!

In December 2023, a reader suggested my idea of share price equal to or less than the income from a $1k investment was too low. I agree with him that opportunities are lost and that many strong stocks exist at higher prices. For example, even the dividend from $10k invested in NVIDIA Corp could not buy one share of that stock. Plainly nobody buys NVDA for the dividend, just growth. (On 3/27/26 $1.00 of NVDA dividend was priced at $4,184.10)

However, I was reminded of a comment in May, 2024 that some readers take “dividends from one $1K invested” to mean the annual return from just one share. To clarify: if the single share price of a stock is $20 and the annual yield is 3.00%, the dividend paid from $1,000.00 invested is $30.00 or 1.5 times the single share price.

February 17, 2025 reader, waxOn, suggested that stock splits could also make dividends from $1k invested exceed the single share price of the stock.

Stay tuned. I now include stock splits in my suggestions to those who determine the price of dividends.

This reader report from CEFreeman.com as of May, 2025 confirms the best use of my advice:

“Interesting! Not only have I discovered I am an accidental Income Investor, but evidently I am also a Dog Catcher. If I am understanding this correctly. I don't have $1000 to invest each time, but I strive to buy enough of something so the dividends always buy 1 share or more. Usually, I try to buy 100 shares. Sure, the positions I buy are very low priced, but I must be doing something right. I am growing my income solidly, penny by penny! Dog Catching Income Investor. I like it.”

The point of this ReFa/Ro article is: “Be More Dog!” Stay tuned, Toy Dog stock breeds have launched for you along with my Top Dogs of each month, quarter and year for the past ten years!

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Brokers Expect 24.15% To 63.09% Net Gains From Top-Ten ReFa/Ro Dogs By March, 2027.

Five of ten top ReFa/Ro Dogs (tinted gray in the chart below) were among the top ten price gainers for the coming year based on analyst 1-year target prices. So, this March, 2026 yield-based forecast for ReFa/Ro dogs, as graded by Wall St. Wizard estimates, was 50% accurate.

Estimated dividend-returns from $1000 invested and aggregate one-year analyst median target prices, for the ten highest-yielding stocks (as reported by YCharts) created the 2026-27 data points for the projections below. (Note: one-year target-prices estimated by lone analysts were not applied.) Ten probable profit-generating trades projected to March, 2027 were:

MFA Financial (MFA) was projected to net $630.87, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 7 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 54% greater than the market as a whole.

Franklin BSP Realty Trust (FBRT) was projected to net $586.83, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 5 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 30% greater than the market as a whole.

Omnicom Group (OMC) was projected to net $351.14, based on dividends, plus median target price estimates from 10 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 27% less than the market as a whole.

Smurfit WestRock (SW) was projected to net $346.51, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 15 analysts, less broker fees. A Beta number was not available for SW.

Alexandria Real Estate (ARE) was projected to net $329.06 based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 16 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 33% greater than the market as a whole.

Kilroy Realty (KRC) was projected to net $266.46, based on annual dividends, the median of target price estimates from 17 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 9% over the market as a whole.

Robert Half (RHI) was projected to net $265.02 based on the median of target prices estimated by 11 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 15% less than the market as a whole.

Ellington Financial (EFC) was projected to net $264.85, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 2 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 69% under the market as a whole.

VICI Properties (VICI) was projected to net $264.68, based on the median of target price estimates from 24 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 30% less than the market as a whole.

Energy Transfer LP (ET) was projected to net $241.47 based on dividends, plus median target price estimates from 21 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 38% less than the market as a whole.

The average net gain in dividend and price was estimated at 35.47% on $10k invested as $1k in each of these top ten ReFa/Ro stocks. This gain estimate was subject to average risk/volatility 15% less than the market as a whole.

The Dividend Dogs Rule

Stocks are dividend dogs when they show three key qualities: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends, and (2) their prices fall. Thus, (3) yield (dividend/price) grows higher than their peers. Thus, the highest yielding stocks in any collection become valued as ”dividend dogs." More specifically, they may be, in fact, best named "underdogs,” owing to their contrarian nature.

The March 2026 ReFa/Ro By Analyst Targets

The March, 2026 ReFa/Ro By Yield

March ReFa/Ro Top 10 Stocks By Yield Ranged 7.40% To 19.73% Per YCharts

Five financial representatives placed first, third through fifth, and eighth: Orchid Island Capital (ORC) [1], MFA Financial [3], AGNC Investment (AGNC) [4], Ellington Financial [5], and Franklin BSP Trust [8].
