data.title: Sezzle: Positive Growth Momentum And An Improved Valuation
data.description:
This is a follow-up article for Sezzle (SEZL), which I last covered in June 2025. I warned investors in that last article that Sezzle's valuation was stretched and that the technicals looked bearish. The stock did decline by over 50% from when that article was published to November 2025.

Sezzle looks much better now from a valuation, growth, and technical perspective. The company is achieving strong growth through its strategies. All of this should help drive the stock higher.

Sezzle's Launch of Virtual Card in Canada

Sezzle's recent launch of its virtual card in Canada could be a positive catalyst for the stock. The virtual card allows users to have a buy now, pay later payment option. This is important because 74% of Sezzle users reported being more likely to shop in-store if Sezzle was available, according to a 2025 survey of Sezzle users.

This card can help to drive traffic and sales to brick-and-mortar retail stores. Then, consumers benefit by having a convenient payment option. So, it should be a win-win situation for consumers and businesses.

Consumers can easily add the Sezzle virtual card to their Apple Pay (AAPL) or Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) wallets. Businesses can easily benefit as well since over 90% of Canadian retailers already accept contactless payments.

Future Growth Outlook

Sezzle finished 2025 on a strong note as revenue increased 66% yoy to $450 million for the full year. Net income increased 69.5% yoy to $133 million. Sezzle achieved strong margin expansion for the year, which helped drive the strong gains in net income. The gross margin increased 62% in 2024 up to 73% in 2025. The operating margin increased from 51% to 59%, and the net income margin increased from 29% to 29.6% over the same time.

The margin gains were attributed to Sezzle's rigorous cost discipline. The company reduced non-transactional operating expenses from 30.4% in 2024 down to 26.3% in 2025. Of course, SEZL's strong revenue growth also had a positive impact on margins. Sezzle sees more opportunity to continue to scale going forward. This is a result of SEZL's core infrastructure acting as a force multiplier. Sezzle has the ability to grow revenue and margins faster than its overhead. Sezzle found a way to finance optimally to fund its growth in a cost-efficient manner through a revolving credit facility.

The company also has an ongoing antitrust suit that Sezzle can't discuss in detail since it is currently in progress. Sezzle did point out that they are seeing positive signs of the environment shifting. SEZL is pleased to see recent regulatory momentum. Sezzle did point out that they are in the discovery phase supported by external consultants and attorneys.

Sezzle's focus on subscription products is meant to drive long-term future growth. This is based on the company's analysis, which demonstrated that subscription users have higher lifetime values than on-demand users. SEZL pointed out that the subscribers made a commitment to the company when they chose to subscribe. Sezzle's subscriber growth increased 30% yoy in Q4 2025 and 18% sequentially. So, the company is achieving positive momentum.

Sezzle attributes disciplined targeted marketing as the strategy to achieve strong returns on investment. The company's current performance demonstrates a 6-month payback on its investments.

Sezzle's success with its returns is evident in its profitability metrics. Sezzle achieved an ROIC of 66% in 2025. The ROE was also strong at 103% last year, while the ROA was 33%. These all significantly outperformed the sector median ROIC of 7%, the ROE of 11%, and the ROA of 1.2%. Sezzle's strong returns can help drive earnings growth going forward. SEZL's returns quantify the effectiveness of the targeted marketing strategy.

Of course, Sezzle's efficiencies aren't only isolated to its targeted marketing. The company evaluates the entire organization for efficiencies and scaling opportunities. Another example of this is the use of AI to improve the customer experience. Sezzle believes that they won't be negatively impacted the way many SaaS companies have been. SEZL believes that the only way the company gets harmed by AI is if they don't enable it.

Sezzle is actually using as much AI as it can to increase efficiencies. SEZL stated that, as an example, they aim to use AI to turn their team of 400 into the equivalent of a team of 4,000 through efficiencies. SEZL is becoming a creator of AI by building its own proprietary engines instead of relying on outside vendors. Sezzle's use of AI enables the company to eliminate expensive 3rd-party costs while improving each aspect of its business. This includes the importance of improving consumer engagement to increase customer retention.

The company has evolved from just a Pay-in-4 product to an all-in-one consumer app. The consumer app now provides financial tools and shopping features designed to help consumers quickly find specific products at the optimal price with the best payment terms for their budget.

Sezzle is guiding for strong 2026 results. The company expects revenue to increase 25% to 30% in 2026. SEZL projects net income to rise about 28% to $170 million and EPS to increase 31% to $4.70. The guidance reflects Sezzle's confidence in continued momentum for its core business. It does not reflect any benefit from new products currently being developed. So, any new product launches could add to these estimates. This strong growth has the potential to catalyze the stock for strong gains if Sezzle meets/exceeds these estimates.

Valuation

At first glance, Sezzle appears to be trading at a premium. This is due to SEZL's forward PE of 15.8x, which is higher than the sector median of 10.5x. However, Sezzle's growth rate is higher than the forward PE. So, I feel that using the PEG ratio would be more appropriate. SEZL is trading with a one-year PEG of just 0.51 (forward PE of 15.8/2026 estimated EPS growth of 30.92%). A PEG below one indicates a bargain, as Sezzle's growth rate is much higher than the forward PE. For context, the sector median PEG is higher at 1.05.

The chart above illustrates Sezzle's stock price (purple line) vs. the EPS growth (orange line). SEZL's EPS has been on a strong uptrend. There was a point last year when the stock price got ahead of the EPS trendline. That reflected an overvaluation. However, the stock is now significantly trailing the EPS trendline. This shows an undervaluation, as the stock price needs to catch up to the EPS trend.

Technical Perspective

Sezzle's daily chart shows the strong rally that the stock experienced this week. All five days had gains. This brought the stock from the $50s up to the $80s within the week.

The RSI indicator is bullish as it moved past 50 and then 60. The RSI remains below the overbought level of 70. So, the stock probably has more room to move higher. Stocks with a lot of positive momentum can move deep into overbought territory.

The MACD is also in bullish mode as the blue line crossed above the red signal line while the histogram bars turned green. The increasing length of the histogram bars indicates rising momentum.

Sezzle's next earnings report for Q1 2026, scheduled for May 6, 2026, could be the next catalyst for the stock. Analysts are expecting Q1 revenue of $129 million and normalized EPS of $1.24. SEZL could meet/exceed these expectations considering the strong momentum that the company has been achieving on the top and bottom lines.

Risks for Sezzle

Sezzle bears all of the credit risk for its customers. Sezzle depends on customers spreading out payments over time. Credit losses are a significant portion of SEZL's operating expenses. Excessive credit failures from customers would have a negative impact on the company.

The buy-now, pay-later industry is subject to various regulations and scrutiny. Increased regulations for the industry could add to Sezzle's costs. More regulations could hinder Sezzle's ability to grow the business.

Competition remains an ongoing risk for Sezzle. The company competes with various payment apps and platforms. The industry has a low barrier to entry. So, competition could increase relatively easily with new entrants in the industry. Increased competition could take market share away from Sezzle and make it more difficult for the company to grow.

Sezzle's Investment Outlook

Sezzle's positive growth momentum has a good chance to continue for the foreseeable future. The company has a solid strategy with its subscription model. Sezzle's above-average expected revenue and earnings growth for 2026 has the potential to drive the stock for above-average gains. Sezzle's low valuation leaves more room to the upside for the stock. The technicals also support more upside momentum for the stock this year.

data.title: RSPS: Consumer Staples Dashboard For April
data.description:
This monthly article series offers a top-down view of the consumer staples sector based on value, quality, and momentum metrics. It may also help analyze sector ETFs such as the Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLP) and Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight Consumer Staples ETF (RSPS), whose holdings are used to calculate these metrics.

Shortcut

The next two paragraphs in italic describe the dashboard methodology. They are necessary for new readers to understand the metrics. If you are used to this series or if you are short of time, you can skip them and go to the charts.

Base Metrics

I calculate the median value of five fundamental ratios for each industry: Earnings Yield ("EY"), Sales Yield ("SY"), Free Cash Flow Yield ("FY"), Return on Equity ("ROE"), Gross Margin ("GM"). The reference universe includes large companies in the U.S. stock market. The five base metrics are calculated on trailing 12 months. For all of them, higher is better. EY, SY and FY are medians of the inverse of Price/Earnings, Price/Sales and Price/Free Cash Flow. They are better for statistical studies than price-to-something ratios, which are unusable or non available when the "something" is close to zero or negative (for example, companies with negative earnings). I also look at two momentum metrics for each group: the median monthly return (RetM) and the median annual return (RetY).

I prefer medians to averages because a median splits a set in a good half and a bad half. A capital-weighted average is skewed by extreme values and the largest companies. My metrics are designed for stock-picking rather than index investing.

Value and Quality Scores

I calculate historical baselines for all metrics. They are noted respectively EYh, SYh, FYh, ROEh, GMh, and they are calculated as the averages on a look-back period of 11 years. For example, the value of EYh for food in the table below is the 11-year average of the median Earnings Yield in food companies.

The Value Score ("VS") is defined as the average difference in % between the three valuation ratios (EY, SY, FY) and their baselines (EYh, SYh, FYh). The same way, the Quality Score ("QS") is the average difference between the two quality ratios (ROE, GM) and their baselines (ROEh, GMh).

The scores are in percentage points. VS may be interpreted as the percentage of undervaluation or overvaluation relative to the baseline (positive is good, negative is bad). This interpretation must be taken with caution: the baseline is an arbitrary reference, not a supposed fair value. The formula assumes that the three valuation metrics are of equal importance.

data.title: AEVEX: Drone Player Taking Off
data.description:
Shares of AEVEX (AVEX) have seen a spectacular move higher on the first day of trading, with investors liking the prospect of this drone producer. Technological and competitive developments are unfolding very rapidly here, making it difficult to see which company and technology has the upper hand, as AEVEX benefits from strong momentum here.

I like the momentum displayed by the business, as the company is seeing strong sales growth, higher margins, and trades at relatively compelling valuation multiples, even after a big jump on the first day of trading. This makes me upbeat as I see appeal, that mostly being relative appeal. I am willing to take a small speculative position, with an interest to learn more about the performance of the business.

Other interesting IPOs are discussed more extensively at Value In Corporate Events.

Enabling Future Of UAS Dominance Strategy

AEVEX has a mission to partner with the US and its international government partners, designed to deliver advanced autonomous systems that ensure success on the battlefield while shaping the future of defense.

The company describes itself as a defense technology prime contractor to enable US Unmanned Aerial Systems (also known as UAS) strategy missions.

The company provides such solutions under two segments. The first are tactical systems, which are mostly used in tactical and actual battle situations, complemented by the global solutions business, used in specific missions. Tactical solutions are responsible for about three-quarters of sales, complemented by the smaller global solutions business.

The company serves a multitude of customers and platforms with over 10,000 systems delivered and committed through the end of this year, comprised of 6,200 deliveries made already and the remainder committed through the rest of this year. Its 100,000-square-foot production facilities have a production capacity of 1,000 units per month, ample space to rack up production here.

The company claims expertise under some key characteristics, including rapid innovation and development cycles, proprietary AI autonomy, aligned software and platforms, hardware integration, and both reliability and predictable production.

Valuation & IPO Thoughts

AEVEX aimed to sell 16 million shares in a preliminary price range between $18 and $21 per share, with pricing seen just over the midpoint of the range at $20 per share. All the shares were sold by the company, which ended up fetching $320 million in gross proceeds; that is excluding the greenshoe option.

With some 112 million shares outstanding following the offering, the company is awarded a $2.24 billion equity valuation at the offer price. The company operates with a net cash position of around $60 million, based on the pro forma balance sheet, for a $2.2 billion enterprise valuation. However, I see $335 million in mezzanine equity on the balance sheet as well, also defined as non-controlling interest on the balance sheet.

While the company sounds like an incredible growth story, it requires some explanation. Revenues rose some 10% in 2025 to $433 million, while gross profits were down 15% to $94 million, marking serious deleverage on that front. The latter is attributed to start-up costs for a new platform to be developed.

Operating income was reported at $8 million after a combined $19 million in amortization charges and contingent consideration, with an adjusted operating profit of $27 million resulting in thin margins. This compares to $63 million operating profit based on the same definition in 2024.

With shares having seen 35% gains on the first day of trading, at a current level of $27 per share, the enterprise valuation has risen to $3.0 billion, or about $3.3 billion if mezzanine equity is included. That results in a roughly 7.5 times sales multiple, yet these are low-margin activities here. However, the 2025 results tell only part of the story, with the funded backlog essentially tripling between the end of 2024 and 2025, reported at just over half a billion.

Moreover, the quarterly revenue cadence in 2025 has been promising, with revenues reported at $53 million, $101 million, $121 million, and $157 million, respectively, during the year. Based on the fourth quarter, revenues trend around $600 million each year, with realistic operating profits trending around $100 million, as that yields a 5-6 times multiple and about 30-33 times operating profit multiple.

Very interesting are the preliminary first-quarter results, which indicate revenues between $200-$208 million, for a run rate around $800 million, or a 4x sales multiple. Adjusted EBITDA is seen around $35 million, for margins in the high-teens, all looking quite encouraging.

Concluding Thoughts

Risks in the offering are plentiful. The biggest ones (in my view) include customer concentration (with the US government making up 80% of sales), reliance on military spending, technology development, stiff competition, complexity, potential for errors, and others. Notably, competition and technological changes are a real risk.

Competition includes many established defense names, but also pure-play companies like AeroVironment (AVAV) as frankly a great deal of cost-effective innovation seems to be developed in active war zones, notably Ukraine. A larger peer like AeroVironment, which I covered recently, is valued around $10 billion, valued at around a 6 times sales multiple. This peer posts EBITDA margins around 10%. In that light, AEVEX trades at a big relative discount in terms of sales multiples, EBITDA multiples, while its growth rates are superior.

This makes me upbeat, certainly from a relative perspective. The spectacular sales growth, with revenues quadrupling and trading around 4 times sales, makes me relatively upbeat. Despite the huge opening day returns, a small speculative position seems warranted. To build conviction in the investment thesis, I would need to be more comfortable with the sustainable pace of growth and operational leverage to be demonstrated upon, yet a small position acting as a watch function seems fine here.

data.title: Beazer Homes USA: Downgrading Is The Right Choice Due To Leverage And Market Concerns
data.description:
One of my favorite things about value investing is the fact that, even when fundamentals are worsening, you can experience upside if a stock was purchased at a low enough price. A great example of this can be seen by looking at Beazer Homes USA (BZH). Back in November of last year, I told investors that the plunge in share price that the company experienced offered an opportunity for upside. I ended up rating it a ‘buy’ in that article. Since then, the stock has risen 9%, meaningfully outperforming the 3.1% increase that the S&P 500 saw. This is in spite of the fact that fundamentals have continued to worsen for the business. In the near term, we could see further deterioration caused by difficult market conditions. And I do think investors would be wise to take that seriously. I wouldn't go so far as to say that this deterioration deserves pessimism when it comes to this particular name. But I do think that, after experiencing the bump up in share price, downgrading it to a ‘hold’ would be prudent, especially in light of the overall balance sheet situation of the business.

Of course, it is always possible that I am being premature and downgrading it. However, I am not the only one who seems to be cautious. Later this month, management will be announcing financial results for the second quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. The expectation that analysts have is that revenue and profitability will worsen. If something big comes out of the woodwork, something that justifies more optimism, I will happily upgrade the stock once again. But for now, I think that taking the more cautious approach with my ‘hold’ rating is justified by the data.

No Longer a Great Home for Your Money

The newest data that investors have regarding Beazer Homes USA covers through the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. In fact, this is the only new data that we have available. During that time, revenue for the company came in at $363.5 million. This unfortunately represents a decline of 22.5% compared to the $469 million that the business reported a year earlier. This was in spite of the fact that the average price of a home sold by the business rose from $507,600 to $513,900. And it was driven, according to management, by a plunge in the number of homes closed from 907 to 700. As revenue dropped, profitability declined. The chart above shows that net profits and cash flows worsened across the board for the company.

This is not the only data point to worsen for the business. As the number of net new orders dropped from 932 to 763, the number of units in backlog for the company declined from 1,507 to 1,008. The only thing helping to offset this from a forward-looking perspective is the fact that the average price of a home in backlog increased from $541,500 to $568,700. Management has been very deliberate about how they are approaching these challenging market conditions. The firm is pushing, for instance, to boost its margins by 300 basis points throughout the year. A continued differentiation is being made by management as they continue to focus on their fully solar communities that they claim already have strong demand and higher margins. This actually makes a lot of sense when you consider that the appeal of such homes would be utility savings over the long haul. But at a time when consumers are under pressure, you would expect higher prices to push demand lower. And sure enough, that is what we have seen.

The good news is that Beazer Homes USA is not in this boat alone. The home building industry in general is under pressure right now. Years of inflationary pressures and high interest rates, not to mention now a weakening economy and the impact of tariffs, have caused home prices to fall as demand drops. Housing completions, for instance, ended up dropping 7.5% in January of this year compared to the same time last year. It is true that housing starts were 9.5% above what they were the year before. But it is my assertion that this is the result of home builders trying to front-load construction activity in order to boost cash flows in the near term as they try and weather the storm and reduce the risk of rising cancellation rates.

More telling, unfortunately, is the fact that building permits ended up dropping 5.8% year over year in January. And this is in spite of the fact that interest rates have declined over the last year, which should in theory push demand up. We are starting to see cancellation rates rise for the company also. From the first quarter of 2025 to the first quarter of 2026, they popped from 16.5% to 18.3%. This follows a drop from 17.7% in 2024 to 17.3% in 2025.

On April 30th, management will be announcing financial results for the second quarter of the year. They expect around 1,100 new home orders and 800 closings. For context, in 2025 for the second quarter, the number of homes delivered came in at 1,089, and the number of homes ordered totaled 1,098. So even though the company is guiding toward a similar number of home orders, closings will be weaker year over year.

Speaking of the second quarter, in the table above, you can see certain results that the company achieved for the 2025 fiscal year. During the second quarter, revenue was $565.3 million. But because of the expectation that closings will decline, analysts believe that sales will come in at only $448.4 million this year. This would mean a drop in earnings per share from $0.42 to negative $0.72, taking the company from a net profit of $12.8 million to a loss of $20.8 million. The table also shows certain cash flow metrics for the second quarter of 2025. If revenue and profitability both do drop this year, then cash flows will almost certainly show signs of deteriorating also.

Because we don't have a firm grasp of what the rest of the year will look like, the best thing that we can do is to value the company in relation to results from 2025. In the chart above, you can see how the firm is priced relative to earnings and cash flows and how other similar companies compare to it. The 2025 approach makes the company look to be somewhere between slightly undervalued and fairly valued. But considering that 2026 will almost certainly be worse than 2025 was, the company will be pushed into the fair value category at this rate.

Relative to other similar firms, the stock seems to be mixed. It's actually cheaper than the other five businesses relative to operating cash flow. But it's around the middle of the group on an EV-to-EBITDA basis, while it is the second most expensive when it comes to the price-to-earnings approach. I did a side, in the chart above, to look at the picture through the lens of the price-to-book multiple. In this respect, the company actually looks dirt cheap, trading at just half of book value. But there is some justification behind this. In the chart below, we can see that it has a net leverage ratio of 6.35. That is well above any of the other five institutions that I compared it to, and it means that the market is discounting the stock in relation to net assets specifically because of the leverage situation that the business is experiencing.

Takeaway

In the past, I was bullish about Beazer Homes USA. But I do think that, in light of changes that we have seen since I last wrote about the company, a more cautious approach is justified now. Based on the data provided, I am becoming a bit concerned, especially considering the amount of leverage the business has. And when you consider that we are nowhere near the bottom in this market, I think it would be prudent to downgrade it to a ‘hold’ at this time.
